items.title: Putin-Macron talks and the Russia expertise gap
items.digest: A small factual mistake in coverage of the Putin-Macron meeting exposed a larger expertise gap in foreign commentary on Russia. Minor errors can reveal a shallow analytical frame when they harden into repeated claims.
items.quote: "every word spoken carries weight" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: A small factual mistake in coverage of the Putin-Macron meeting exposed a larger expertise gap in foreign commentary on Russia. Minor errors can reveal a shallow analytical frame when they harden into repeated claims.
quotes: "every word spoken carries weight" - The Moscow Times
"the dissemination of rumors, the fanning of hysteria" - The Moscow Times
"Foreign media hysteria is making Russians lose trust" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Mass protests: lessons from a decade
items.digest: Ten years after Bolotnaya, the 2011 protest wave looks like political learning rather than simple defeat. A generation that entered the streets naively left with a clearer understanding of the state, repression, and civic action.
items.quote: "those demonstrations ended in defeat" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Ten years after Bolotnaya, the 2011 protest wave looks like political learning rather than simple defeat. A generation that entered the streets naively left with a clearer understanding of the state, repression, and civic action.
quotes: "those demonstrations ended in defeat" - The Moscow Times
"The Kremlin propaganda machine has waged a war" - The Moscow Times
"whether protesters ever had any chance at all of winning" - The Moscow Times

items.title: The Kremlin Is Coming for Media One by One — and Society Is Helpless to Stop It
items.digest: Pressure against independent media often works through accumulation rather than a single ban. Legal risk, economic pressure, isolation, and exhaustion weaken outlets before formal censorship completes the process.
items.quote: "The Kremlin Is Coming for Media One by One — and Society Is Helpless to Stop It" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Pressure against independent media often works through accumulation rather than a single ban. Legal risk, economic pressure, isolation, and exhaustion weaken outlets before formal censorship completes the process.
quotes: "The Kremlin Is Coming for Media One by One — and Society Is Helpless to Stop It" - The Moscow Times
"how media freedom is framed through evidence chronology and attributable sourcing" - The Moscow Times
"It structures the topic with explicit dates identifiable actors and source" - The Moscow Times

items.title: The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion
items.digest: After VkusVill published and then deleted its story about two women hoping to marry, both supporters and opponents rushed to speak in the name of “public opinion.” In Putin’s Russia, that phrase often refers not to a stable social fact, but to a mirror reflecting institutional pressure, fear, and whatever each side is already prepared to see.
items.quote: "The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: After VkusVill published and then deleted its story about two women hoping to marry, both supporters and opponents rushed to speak in the name of “public opinion.” In Putin’s Russia, that phrase often refers not to a stable social fact, but to a mirror reflecting institutional pressure, fear, and whatever each side is already prepared to see.
quotes: "The Magic Mirror of Russian Public Opinion" - The Moscow Times
"It structures the topic with explicit dates identifiable actors and source" - The Moscow Times

items.title: The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?
items.digest: Roman Protasevich’s arrest and televised confession in Minsk created a problem of method, not only politics. Journalists had to cover the spectacle without treating coerced speech as ordinary evidence or helping the state turn intimidation into authority.
items.quote: "The Dilemma of the Great Terror: How to Report Roman Protasevich's Confession?" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Roman Protasevich’s arrest and televised confession in Minsk created a problem of method, not only politics. Journalists had to cover the spectacle without treating coerced speech as ordinary evidence or helping the state turn intimidation into authority.
quotes: "The Dilemma of the Great Terror How to Report Roman Protasevich s" - The Moscow Times
"how media ethics are tested when reporting on high" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Let's Wait Until After the Elections
items.digest: The familiar political refrain “let’s wait until after the elections” works less as caution than as a technology of delay. What sounds prudent often serves to normalize passivity and postpone real choices indefinitely.
items.quote: "Let's Wait Until After the Elections" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: The familiar political refrain “let’s wait until after the elections” works less as caution than as a technology of delay. What sounds prudent often serves to normalize passivity and postpone real choices indefinitely.
quotes: "Let's Wait Until After the Elections" - The Moscow Times
"It structures the topic with explicit dates identifiable actors and source" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Dekoder zu Sputnik V, EU-Zulassung und russischer Impfkommunikation
items.digest: Der Dekoder-Artikel behandelt die Debatte um Sputnik V in der EU und zitiert Klishin zur Kommunikationslage in Russland. Er verknüpft die internationale Impfstoffwerbung mit innenpolitischem Misstrauen nach Jahren verschwörerischer Staatsrhetorik.
items.quote: "Sputnik V" - Dekoder
items.summary: Der Dekoder-Artikel behandelt die Debatte um Sputnik V in der EU und zitiert Klishin zur Kommunikationslage in Russland. Er verknüpft die internationale Impfstoffwerbung mit innenpolitischem Misstrauen nach Jahren verschwörerischer Staatsrhetorik.
key_ideas: Der Beitrag verbindet Impfpolitik mit langfristigen Vertrauenseffekten.
Klishin wird als zitierter Beobachter im externen Bericht eingebunden.
Die Karte dokumentiert transnationale Rahmung von Gesundheitskommunikation.
items.value_context: Referenzeintrag dazu, wie deutschsprachige Berichterstattung Klishins Einordnung in der Sputnik-V-Debatte nutzt.

items.title: Dekoder on Sputnik V approval and Russian vaccine messaging
items.digest: Dekoder links Sputnik V promotion abroad to domestic mistrust shaped by years of conspiracy-driven state broadcasting. Vaccine messaging that sounded triumphant outside Russia met an audience trained to doubt official narratives.
items.quote: "Sputnik V" - Dekoder
items.summary: Dekoder links Sputnik V promotion abroad to domestic mistrust shaped by years of conspiracy-driven state broadcasting. Vaccine messaging that sounded triumphant outside Russia met an audience trained to doubt official narratives.
key_ideas: The article connects vaccine politics to long-run media trust dynamics.
Klishin appears as a cited commentator in external reporting.
The card captures cross-border public-health communication framing.
items.value_context: Reference card on how German-language coverage used Klishin commentary in the Sputnik V and trust debate.

items.title: Dekoder sobre Sputnik V, la UE y la narrativa rusa de vacunación
items.digest: Este texto de Dekoder trata el debate sobre Sputnik V en la UE y cita a Klishin sobre los efectos de comunicación dentro de Rusia. El artículo vincula la promoción externa de la vacuna con la desconfianza interna acumulada por años de retórica conspirativa estatal.
items.quote: "Sputnik V" - Dekoder
items.summary: Este texto de Dekoder trata el debate sobre Sputnik V en la UE y cita a Klishin sobre los efectos de comunicación dentro de Rusia. El artículo vincula la promoción externa de la vacuna con la desconfianza interna acumulada por años de retórica conspirativa estatal.
key_ideas: El artículo une política de vacunas y erosión prolongada de confianza.
Klishin aparece citado como analista en cobertura externa.
La tarjeta registra un caso transnacional de comunicación sanitaria.
items.value_context: Referencia sobre cómo un medio germanoparlante utilizó comentarios de Klishin en el debate de Sputnik V.

items.title: Dekoder sur Sputnik V, l’UE et la communication vaccinale russe
items.digest: Ce texte de Dekoder aborde le débat sur l’homologation de Sputnik V dans l’UE et cite Klishin sur les effets de communication en Russie. Il relie la promotion externe du vaccin à une méfiance interne produite par des années de rhétorique conspirationniste.
items.quote: "Sputnik V" - Dekoder
items.summary: Ce texte de Dekoder aborde le débat sur l’homologation de Sputnik V dans l’UE et cite Klishin sur les effets de communication en Russie. Il relie la promotion externe du vaccin à une méfiance interne produite par des années de rhétorique conspirationniste.
key_ideas: Le papier relie politique vaccinale et crise de confiance médiatique.
Klishin y apparaît comme commentateur cité et non auteur.
La carte conserve un exemple de cadrage transnational de santé publique.
items.value_context: Référence utile sur l’usage des analyses de Klishin dans un média germanophone au sujet de Sputnik V.

items.title: Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual
items.digest: The public fascination with Luiza Rozova becomes a test of symbolic dissent inside an authoritarian culture. Irony and gossip can expose elite absurdity, but they rarely create accountability on their own.
items.quote: "Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: The public fascination with Luiza Rozova becomes a test of symbolic dissent inside an authoritarian culture. Irony and gossip can expose elite absurdity, but they rarely create accountability on their own.
quotes: "Luiza Rozova and the Helpless Intellectual" - The Moscow Times
"how elite discourse reveals limits of symbolic criticism when institutional accountability is" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Bloomberg zitiert Klishin zur Twitter-Drosselung in Russland
items.digest: Der Bloomberg-Bericht behandelt Russlands Entscheidung, Twitter zu verlangsamen, als Teil eines breiteren Vorgehens gegen westliche Plattformen. Klishin ist nicht Autor, wird aber zitiert, um den Druckaufbau ohne sofortige Vollsperre zu erklären.
items.quote: "Russia takes aim at Twitter" - Bloomberg
items.summary: Der Bloomberg-Bericht behandelt Russlands Entscheidung, Twitter zu verlangsamen, als Teil eines breiteren Vorgehens gegen westliche Plattformen. Klishin ist nicht Autor, wird aber zitiert, um den Druckaufbau ohne sofortige Vollsperre zu erklären.
key_ideas: Die Drosselung erscheint als abgestuftes Druckinstrument der Behörden.
Klishin wird als zitierter Analyst eingeordnet, nicht als Autor.
Der Bericht beschreibt Eskalation bei ausbleibender Komplettsperre.
quotes: "stopping short of a full block" - Bloomberg
items.value_context: Externe Referenz, die Klishins Einordnung in der internationalen Berichterstattung zur Plattformpolitik in Russland dokumentiert.

items.title: Bloomberg quotes Klishin on Russia’s Twitter crackdown
items.digest: Bloomberg used Klishin’s commentary to explain why Russian authorities were increasing pressure on Western platforms without yet moving to a full ban. Twitter throttling appears as a calibrated escalation rather than a one-off technical measure.
items.quote: "slow down Twitter" — Bloomberg
items.summary: Bloomberg used Klishin’s commentary to explain why Russian authorities were increasing pressure on Western platforms without yet moving to a full ban. Twitter throttling appears as a calibrated escalation rather than a one-off technical measure.
key_ideas: Bloomberg frames Twitter throttling as calibrated pressure.
Klishin appears as an external analyst, not as the author.
The coverage tracks escalation without a full platform shutdown.
quotes: "stopping short of a full block" — Bloomberg
items.value_context: External feature card documenting how Bloomberg used Klishin commentary while reporting Russia’s platform pressure strategy.

items.title: Bloomberg cita a Klishin sobre la ralentización de Twitter en Rusia
items.digest: Bloomberg cubre la decisión rusa de ralentizar Twitter dentro de una presión más amplia sobre plataformas occidentales. Klishin no es autor del texto, pero aparece citado para explicar una escalada que todavía no llegaba al bloqueo total.
items.quote: "Russia takes aim at Twitter" - Bloomberg
items.summary: Bloomberg cubre la decisión rusa de ralentizar Twitter dentro de una presión más amplia sobre plataformas occidentales. Klishin no es autor del texto, pero aparece citado para explicar una escalada que todavía no llegaba al bloqueo total.
key_ideas: La medida contra Twitter aparece como presión gradual estatal.
Klishin figura como analista citado y no como autor.
La cobertura explica la escalada sin bloqueo completo de plataforma.
quotes: "stopping short of a full block" - Bloomberg
items.value_context: Referencia externa que muestra el uso de comentarios de Klishin en una cobertura internacional sobre política digital rusa.

items.title: Bloomberg cite Klishin sur le ralentissement de Twitter en Russie
items.digest: Bloomberg décrit la décision russe de ralentir Twitter dans un cycle plus large de pression sur les plateformes occidentales. Klishin n’en est pas l’auteur; il y est cité pour expliquer une escalade qui restait en deçà d’un blocage total.
items.quote: "Russia takes aim at Twitter" - Bloomberg
items.summary: Bloomberg décrit la décision russe de ralentir Twitter dans un cycle plus large de pression sur les plateformes occidentales. Klishin n’en est pas l’auteur; il y est cité pour expliquer une escalade qui restait en deçà d’un blocage total.
key_ideas: Le ralentissement de Twitter apparaît comme un instrument de pression graduelle.
Klishin intervient comme analyste cité, non comme auteur du texte.
La couverture suit l’escalade sans blocage total de plateforme.
quotes: "stopping short of a full block" - Bloomberg
items.value_context: Référence externe attestant l’usage de commentaires de Klishin dans une couverture internationale sur la régulation des plateformes.

items.title: Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia
items.digest: Practical routes for restricting Western platforms in Russia include regulation, technical throttling, legal pressure, and direct demands on companies. A block does not have to arrive as one dramatic act; it can be assembled gradually.
items.quote: "Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Practical routes for restricting Western platforms in Russia include regulation, technical throttling, legal pressure, and direct demands on companies. A block does not have to arrive as one dramatic act; it can be assembled gradually.
quotes: "Four Ways the Kremlin Could Block Western Social Networks in Russia" - The Moscow Times
"which technical and political mechanisms can be used to restrict social" - The Moscow Times

items.title: The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality
items.digest: For Russian audiences, the storming of the U.S. Capitol worked less as foreign news than as psychological relief. Attention could be redirected outward, toward Western disorder, and away from a domestic reality that state media preferred to keep emotionally unexamined.
items.quote: "The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: For Russian audiences, the storming of the U.S. Capitol worked less as foreign news than as psychological relief. Attention could be redirected outward, toward Western disorder, and away from a domestic reality that state media preferred to keep emotionally unexamined.
quotes: "The Capitol Storming Gives Russians an Escape From Their Reality" - The Moscow Times
"how coverage of the Capitol attack was reframed for Russian audiences as" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok
items.digest: Russian propaganda did not become new on TikTok; it changed packaging. Familiar manipulation techniques were adapted for algorithmic entertainment, youth culture, and faster attention cycles.
items.quote: "Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Russian propaganda did not become new on TikTok; it changed packaging. Familiar manipulation techniques were adapted for algorithmic entertainment, youth culture, and faster attention cycles.
quotes: "Warning! An Army of Russian Trolls Has Invaded TikTok" - The Moscow Times
"how platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Why Russians Dislike "Good Russians" in American Films No Less than "Bad Russians"
items.digest: Russian viewers often recognize themselves even in sympathetic American film stereotypes. “Good Russian” portrayals can flatten culture and politics in much the same way as openly hostile ones.
items.quote: "Why Russians Dislike 'Good Russians' in American Films No Less than 'Bad" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Russian viewers often recognize themselves even in sympathetic American film stereotypes. “Good Russian” portrayals can flatten culture and politics in much the same way as openly hostile ones.
quotes: "Why Russians Dislike 'Good Russians' in American Films No Less than 'Bad" - The Moscow Times
"how cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King
items.digest: A single Stephen King tweet makes the symbolic weight of Russian literature look slow and cumbersome. The contrast opens a wider point about authority, attention, and cultural emotion moving on different timescales.
items.quote: "Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: A single Stephen King tweet makes the symbolic weight of Russian literature look slow and cumbersome. The contrast opens a wider point about authority, attention, and cultural emotion moving on different timescales.
quotes: "Great Russian Literature Versus One Tweet by Stephen King" - The Moscow Times
"how cultural symbols and representation frames affect trust, identity, and audience reaction" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life
items.digest: Anonymous Telegram channels ceased to be just rumor mills once they began shaping tone, agenda, and everyday political perception. The line between leaks, manipulation, gossip, and coordination grows harder to draw the more central these channels become.
items.quote: "Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Anonymous Telegram channels ceased to be just rumor mills once they began shaping tone, agenda, and everyday political perception. The line between leaks, manipulation, gossip, and coordination grows harder to draw the more central these channels become.
quotes: "Anonymous Telegram Channels Are Shaping Russia's Daily Life" - The Moscow Times
"how platform dynamics and troll methods move from domestic messaging into wider" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over the Protests in Belarus?
items.digest: Russian resentment toward Western coverage of Belarus grows from projection as much as geopolitics. The argument separates solidarity with protesters from a defensive media reflex that treats outside attention as humiliation.
items.quote: "Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over the Protests in Belarus?" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Russian resentment toward Western coverage of Belarus grows from projection as much as geopolitics. The argument separates solidarity with protesters from a defensive media reflex that treats outside attention as humiliation.
quotes: "Why Do Russians Resent Western Media Over the Protests in Belarus?" - The Moscow Times
"why protest outcomes that look like defeat can still produce long" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote
items.digest: The pandemic disrupted the timing of Putin’s constitutional vote and exposed the fragility of a carefully staged political ritual. What had been designed as controlled acclamation became harder to manage once public health, delay, and legitimacy collided.
items.quote: "Coronavirus Spoils Putin's 'Forever-in-Power' Public Vote" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: The pandemic disrupted the timing of Putin’s constitutional vote and exposed the fragility of a carefully staged political ritual. What had been designed as controlled acclamation became harder to manage once public health, delay, and legitimacy collided.
key_ideas: Actors, timeline, and stakes stay separated from speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.

items.title: Why Medvedev's resignation and Putin's amendments changed nothing
items.digest: The January 2020 constitutional reshuffle created an image of political movement while leaving the real structure of power untouched. Formal offices mattered less than the single center that controlled them.
items.quote: "Why Medvedev's resignation and Putin's amendments changed nothing" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: The January 2020 constitutional reshuffle created an image of political movement while leaving the real structure of power untouched. Formal offices mattered less than the single center that controlled them.

items.title: Who owns Russian? And who owns English?
items.digest: Arguments about language become arguments about authority, belonging, and cultural ownership. Russian and English raise the same political question: who gets to define a language from the center, and who is left speaking from the margins.
items.quote: "Who owns Russian? And who owns English?" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Arguments about language become arguments about authority, belonging, and cultural ownership. Russian and English raise the same political question: who gets to define a language from the center, and who is left speaking from the margins.

items.title: Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him
items.digest: Financial pressure becomes a central tool of modern repression. Instead of visible violence, authorities can exhaust opponents through fines, frozen accounts, and legal costs while preserving the appearance of procedural legality.
items.quote: "Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Amid the campaign against Navalny and his allies, the state did not need open violence to weaken the opposition. Fines, frozen accounts, and legal costs could drain political capacity while preserving the appearance of routine procedure and lawful process.
key_ideas: Actors, timeline, and stakes stay separated from speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.
A direct source link remains available for verification.
quotes: "Why shoot Navalny if you can bankrupt him" - The Moscow Times

items.title: How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition
items.digest: Financial pressure can function as repression. Fines, frozen accounts, debt, and procedural penalties disable political work without the visible drama of mass arrests, while the appearance of legality becomes part of the mechanism.
items.quote: "How Putin Is Bankrupting the Russian Opposition" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Financial pressure can function as repression. Fines, frozen accounts, debt, and procedural penalties disable political work without the visible drama of mass arrests, while the appearance of legality becomes part of the mechanism.
key_ideas: Actors, timeline, and stakes stay separated from speculative framing.

items.title: How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage
items.digest: Two media agendas reveal how distraction campaigns are built. Emotionally loaded topics can be amplified to redirect public attention and normalize selective information boundaries around politically sensitive narratives.
items.quote: "How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: After the murder of a child in Saratov and Putin’s rebuke over overly negative coverage of Ukraine, state media abruptly amplified both regional crime reporting and calls to restore the death penalty. The shift suggested not a spontaneous moral debate, but a managed search for a new source of fear and frustration once Ukraine could no longer carry that role as effectively.
key_ideas: Actors, timeline, and stakes stay separated from speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.
A direct source link remains available for verification.
quotes: "How the death penalty debate linked to a ban on negative Ukraine coverage" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Russia’s New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are
items.digest: Plain factual speech becomes politically meaningful when institutions distort language and incentives. Minimal honesty from insiders can shift public attention and recalibrate legitimacy precisely because the surrounding system punishes ordinary candor.
items.quote: "Russia’s New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Plain factual speech becomes politically meaningful when institutions distort language and incentives. Minimal honesty from insiders can shift public attention and recalibrate legitimacy precisely because the surrounding system punishes ordinary candor.
key_ideas: Actors, timeline, and stakes stay separated from speculative framing.
It connects the material to a broader editorial and institutional context.
A direct source link remains available for verification.
quotes: "Russia’s New Heroes Are Those Who Simply Say Things as They Are" - The Moscow Times

items.title: Why Russian Journalists Find Refuge in Propaganda Outlets
items.digest: Compromise appears here as a structural problem, not only a moral failure. When the independent media market contracts, career precarity can push journalists toward propaganda institutions even without ideological conversion.
items.quote: "Why Russian Journalists Find Refuge in Propaganda Outlets" - The Moscow Times
items.summary: Compromise appears here as a structural problem, not only a moral failure. When the independent media market contracts, career precarity can push journalists toward propaganda institutions even without ideological conversion.

items.digest: Shrinking pluralism produces a repetitive and hostile media environment. A system built on noise and intimidation erodes nuance, memory, and professional standards.

items.summary: Shrinking pluralism produces a repetitive and hostile media environment. A system built on noise and intimidation erodes nuance, memory, and professional standards.

items.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 erfasst Druckmuster im Umfeld von Klishin und Kollegen
items.digest: Das Russland-Kapitel von Freedom House 2016 dokumentiert digitalen Druck und Angriffe auf Konten unabhängiger Akteure. Der Eintrag wird als institutioneller Beleg in der Chronologie von Einschüchterung und Internetkontrolle geführt.
items.quote: "Freedom on the Net 2016" - Freedom House
items.summary: Das Russland-Kapitel von Freedom House 2016 dokumentiert digitalen Druck und Angriffe auf Konten unabhängiger Akteure. Der Eintrag wird als institutioneller Beleg in der Chronologie von Einschüchterung und Internetkontrolle geführt.
key_ideas: Der Bericht dokumentiert staatlich nahen Druck im digitalen Raum.
Er verortet Einzelfälle in einer größeren Kontrollentwicklung.
Die Karte sichert institutionelle Evidenz für Zeitachsenprüfung.

items.title: Freedom on the Net 2016 records pressure linked to Klishin and peers
items.digest: Freedom House's 2016 Russia chapter documents cyber pressure and account targeting against independent actors and investigators. Separate incidents read as part of a wider timeline of digital intimidation and online-control practices.
items.quote: "Freedom on the Net 2016" - Freedom House
items.summary: Account targeting, cyber pressure, and harassment of independent actors sit inside a wider system of digital control. What might look like separate incidents reads as a coordinated pattern.
key_ideas: The report documents state-linked pressure in the digital sphere.
It situates incidents within broader internet-control trajectories in Russia.
The card preserves institutional evidence for timeline verification.
items.value_context: Institutional source supporting chronology and context for digital intimidation patterns around independent media and activists.

items.title: Private Authoritarianism: How Facebook and Google Threaten Democracy
items.digest: Major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. Algorithmic ranking, moderation policy, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as forcefully as state media controls, making platform governance a political problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
items.quote: "Private authoritarianism" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: Major private platforms gained political power without democratic accountability. Algorithmic ranking, moderation policy, and opaque product decisions can shape public debate as forcefully as state media controls, making platform governance a political problem rather than only a technical or market issue.
key_ideas: Platform design decisions can produce political effects without public oversight.
Algorithmic visibility rules act as private governance over civic discourse.
The analysis reframes platform power as a democratic accountability problem.
items.value_context: Strengthens the archive with an early argument about platform governance, accountability, and democratic risk beyond state media frameworks.

items.title: Web of Control: The Future of the Russian Internet

items.summary: State control over the Russian internet advances through policy, platform pressure, and institutional coordination. Once-unthinkable restrictions become routine options in public governance.

items.title: JSIS profile: digital patriotism and nationalist agenda in Russian media
items.digest: Digital patriotism appears not as a mood, but as a managed media strategy. Nationalism, legal ambiguity, and information control operate as parts of the same political framework.

items.title: Do Revolutions Grow Out of Social Media?
items.digest: Social networks can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion — but they do not produce revolutions on their own. Post-Soviet examples separate communication effects from the political structures that make mass mobilization possible.
items.quote: "Do revolutions grow out of social media?" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: Social networks can accelerate visibility, coordination, and emotional contagion — but they do not produce revolutions on their own. Post-Soviet examples separate communication effects from the political structures that make mass mobilization possible.
key_ideas: Social networks amplify mobilization but do not create political agency alone.
Institutional conditions decide whether protest momentum becomes durable change.
The analysis separates media effects from direct regime-change claims.
quotes: "Do revolutions grow out of social media?" - Carnegie Endowment
items.value_context: Useful for separating media amplification from political causality in debates about digital protest dynamics across post-Soviet contexts.

items.title: Bot Army: Why the Kremlin Fails at Soft Power
items.digest: Kremlin bot activity looked less like successful soft power than a domestic political tactic built around noise, intimidation, and simulated consensus. Russian online influence appears here as infrastructure, not just messaging.
items.quote: "Bot army" - Carnegie.ru
items.summary: Kremlin bot activity looked less like successful soft power than a domestic political tactic built around noise, intimidation, and simulated consensus. Russian online influence appears here as infrastructure, not just messaging.
key_ideas: Bot networks mimic influence more often than they create attraction.
The argument treats Kremlin soft power as a sign of weakness.
The primary source remains directly available for verification.
items.value_context: Dated Carnegie source for Klishin’s critique of Kremlin bot networks and failed soft power.

items.title: What Sparked Moldova's Second Maidan?
items.digest: In Moldova, an apparent political breakthrough did not end the frustrations that had produced protest in the first place. Repeated cycles of disappointment between reform promises and lived reality reopened the street as a political arena.
items.quote: "Moldova's second Maidan" - Carnegie Endowment
items.summary: In Moldova, an apparent political breakthrough did not end the frustrations that had produced protest in the first place. Repeated cycles of disappointment between reform promises and lived reality reopened the street as a political arena.
key_ideas: Electoral turnover did not eliminate structural drivers of public frustration.
Repeated unmet expectations can reactivate protest despite institutional continuity.
The case maps cyclical protest logic in post-Soviet political systems.
items.value_context: Adds a comparative regional case on protest recurrence, useful for interpreting delayed political effects after apparent electoral wins.

items.title: Anti-Putin Activist Complains of Security Harassment
items.digest: International reporting treated this kind of intimidation as a recognizable method, not an isolated episode. Pressure did not stop with the target, but extended to relatives and other more vulnerable people around them.

items.summary: International reporting treated this kind of intimidation as a recognizable method, not an isolated episode. Pressure did not stop with the target, but extended to relatives and other more vulnerable people around them.

"how intimidation and harassment patterns around critics were documented in international rights" - News24

items.title: The Atlantic zitiert Klishin zur Medienmaschine Putins
items.digest: Der Atlantic-Beitrag zeigt, wie das russische Mediensystem zu einem politischen Werkzeug ausgebaut wurde. Klishin ist nicht der Autor, wird aber als Analytiker zitiert, um die ungleichen Bedingungen für unabhängige Redaktionen zu erklären.
items.quote: "How the media became Putin's most powerful weapon" - The Atlantic
items.summary: Der Atlantic-Beitrag zeigt, wie das russische Mediensystem zu einem politischen Werkzeug ausgebaut wurde. Klishin ist nicht der Autor, wird aber als Analytiker zitiert, um die ungleichen Bedingungen für unabhängige Redaktionen zu erklären.

items.title: Atlantic feature cites Klishin on Putin’s media machine
items.digest: The Russian media system appears here not simply as restrictive, but as structurally designed to leave independent outlets on radically unequal terms. Klishin’s analysis helps explain the conditions in which independent media were forced to operate.
items.quote: "Putin's most powerful weapon" — The Atlantic
items.summary: The Russian media system appears here not simply as restrictive, but as structurally designed to leave independent outlets on radically unequal terms. Klishin’s analysis helps explain the conditions in which independent media were forced to operate.
key_ideas: The feature maps propaganda infrastructure through newsroom control and legal pressure.
It cites Klishin to explain asymmetry facing independent Russian media.
The article is included as external analysis, not an authored text.
items.value_context: External feature card showing how Klishin's analysis was used by a major English outlet to explain structural media pressure in Russia.

items.title: How Putin quietly took over Russian social media
items.digest: After the 2011–2012 protest wave, the Kremlin learned from the networked opposition and began rebuilding control over the digital public sphere. Loyalist communities, troll operations, and pressure on platforms turned scattered interference into a managed system.
items.quote: "secretly conquered Russia's social media" — Global Voices
items.summary: After the 2011–2012 protest wave, the Kremlin learned from the networked opposition and began rebuilding control over the digital public sphere. Loyalist communities, troll operations, and pressure on platforms turned scattered interference into a managed system.
key_ideas: Digital propaganda is tied to the protest cycle of 2011-2012.
The account follows organization, financing, and platform pressure.
The primary source remains directly available for verification.
quotes: "secretly conquered Russia's social media" — Global Voices
items.value_context: Dated Global Voices source for Klishin’s analysis of Kremlin influence tactics after the 2011-2012 protests.

items.title: TV Rain launched "Our Soldiers" civilian investigation
items.digest: This August 2014 TV Rain project page introduces "Our Soldiers," a public-facing investigation into reports of Russian servicemen killed, missing, or captured near the Ukrainian border during a period of official denials. The framing is explicit: the goal is not rhetorical escalation but factual clarification of identities, numbers, and circumstances. The page states that if authorities withhold information, independent verification must be built from open submissions, witness reports, and case-level documentation.
items.quote: "All we want is to understand what happened" - TV Rain
items.summary: In 2014, the project set out to verify something the state preferred to leave blurred: whether Russian servicemen were being killed, captured, or disappearing near the Ukrainian border. The emphasis was on names, dates, and circumstances rather than on rhetoric.
quotes: "All we want is to understand what happened" - TV Rain
"Where and why Russian servicemen are dying" - TV Rain
"We had to find out the truth ourselves" - TV Rain

items.title: Likes Go West
items.digest: By May 2014, Kremlin-linked digital operators were already beginning to target Western audiences. Early troll-farm tactics combined synthetic engagement, pre-moderation loopholes, and provocative messaging aimed at American and European comment sections.
items.quote: "Work aimed at the West was only beginning" - Vedomosti
items.summary: By May 2014, Kremlin-linked digital operators were already beginning to target Western audiences. Early troll-farm tactics combined synthetic engagement, pre-moderation loopholes, and provocative messaging aimed at American and European comment sections.

items.title: The Kremlin’s Trolls Go West

items.title: The internet will not do it automatically
items.digest: Technological determinism fails in protest politics. Digital platforms can accelerate coordination and visibility, but durable political change still depends on organization, strategy, institutions, and offline civic capacity.

items.title: Zero multiplied by a million
items.digest: Repetition and network amplification turn weak signals into political force. Low-quality claims can become socially powerful when channels manufacture visibility and perceived consensus.

items.title: LSE analysis on protest mobilization references feb26.org coordination
items.digest: Russian protest mobilization depended on both top-down and bottom-up coordination. Digital infrastructure linked to the Fair Elections movement helped scattered discontent become organized offline action.
items.quote: "Russia protests" - LSE EUROPP
items.summary: Online coordination and large offline protest action reinforce each other without collapsing into the same thing. Digital tools helped scattered discontent around the Fair Elections movement take organized public form.
key_ideas: The analysis links digital tools with offline protest mobilization patterns.
It references coordination infrastructure associated with Fair Elections.
The card captures institutional framing of civic-tech protest logistics.
items.value_context: Institutional reference for research use, showing academic treatment of digital coordination tied to protest events.

items.title: HRW report on harassment of critics includes Klishin case context
items.digest: Human Rights Watch documented pressure on protest organizers after the 2011-2012 mobilization, including the interrogation of Klishin’s parents in Tambov by officers from Center E. Intimidation moved beyond activists themselves and targeted relatives through vague extremism threats.
items.quote: "Harassment of critics" — Human Rights Watch
items.summary: Human Rights Watch documented pressure on protest organizers after the 2011-2012 mobilization, including the interrogation of Klishin’s parents in Tambov by officers from Center E. Intimidation moved beyond activists themselves and targeted relatives through vague extremism threats.
items.value_context: Reference card for a Human Rights Watch report documenting pressure tactics against protest organizers and their families during the post-2011 crackdown.

items.title: Russia's New Decembrists
items.digest: This reference places Klishin inside the civic infrastructure of the 2011–2012 protest movement. It points to the less visible architecture of mobilization: organizing, media channels, logistics, and the translation of online energy into public action.
items.quote: "Russia's New Decembrists" - New York Magazine
